summary: Outcome-oriented teams are mandated and equipped to deliver business outcomes rather than just completing activities or delivering scope. Unlike activity-oriented teams that can only perform single specialized functions, outcome-oriented teams are cross-functional and self-sufficient. True outcome realization requires iterative collaboration between problem-understanders and solution-builders, which fails when product management is separated from development. While outcome orientation works better than activity orientation for large projects, the project-centric funding model limits its effectiveness by disbanding teams before outcomes fully materialize.
key_points: Business sponsors care about outcomes (lower effort, better conversion, customer satisfaction) not development metrics like velocity or deployment frequency
Outcome-oriented teams require cross-functional composition with all capabilities needed to deliver results, unlike mono-functional activity teams
Delivering scope is easy; delivering outcomes requires iterative collaboration where solution attempts improve problem understanding
Communities of practice with dedicated leads help mitigate the mentorship gap when people of the same specialty are distributed across teams
Project-centric funding undermines outcome orientation by disbanding teams before outcomes fully materialize in the business
money_quotes: People who sponsor development of software usually aren't very interested in development metrics such as velocity or frequency of deployment to production.
Scope delivery is easy, relatively speaking. Outcome realization requires real collaboration between those who understand the problem and those who can fashion various levels of solution for it.
Initial attempts at solution lead to a better understanding of the problem which leads to further attempts at better solutions.
This doesn't work where the product management organization is separate from the development (scope-delivery) organization.
A mandate to deliver a business outcome is very different from a mandate to deliver a certain amount of scope.
They are disbanded at the end of the project which means we lose sight of the outcome until another release in the same area is funded with another team.
key_insight: True outcome delivery requires cross-functional teams with both the mandate and capability to iterate on solutions, which traditional activity-oriented and project-centric structures inherently prevent.
